issue_title: Can't enable delete from notification button in settings

reasoning_trace.finding: The preferred search tool `rg` was unavailable in this environment, so subsequent searches used local PowerShell file searches.
Confirmed this is the Thunderbird for Android/K-9 Mail white-label repository and that K-9 Mail is built from shared code in this checkout.
Confirmed local contribution conventions and that Android/Kotlin project code should be investigated in the checked-out modules.

reasoning_trace.target: notificationQuickDelete, NotificationQuickDelete, isDeleteActionEnabled, QuickDelete, Notification actions, notification actions
reasoning_trace.finding: Found the exact reported guard in `legacy/core/src/main/java/com/fsck/k9/notification/SingleMessageNotificationDataCreator.kt` lines 122-123, preference storage in `core/preference`, legacy setting descriptions, and the notification actions settings UI in `legacy/ui/legacy`.
Confirmed module roles: app modules, core preference modules, feature modules, and legacy modules. Notification and settings code involved here is in legacy UI/core plus core preference.

Confirmed API/internal boundary rules; relevant preference contracts live in core preference API, implementation in core preference impl/internal-style module, and legacy notification code consumes the API.

`createSingleNotificationActions()` parses configured action order and cutoff, then calls `resolveActions(..., isDeleteEnabled = isDeleteActionEnabled(), ...)`. `isDeleteActionEnabled()` returns false when `notificationQuickDeleteBehaviour == NotificationQuickDelete.NEVER`, causing `NotificationAction.Delete` to be filtered out even if present in configured notification actions.

Summary notification delete uses `isSummaryDeleteActionEnabled`, not the single-message action order directly. This is a neighboring path and does not explain the configured single-message notification action disappearing.

`getString()` still exposes `notification_quick_delete` from `notificationSettings.notificationQuickDeleteBehaviour.name`, but `putString()` has no branch for `notification_quick_delete`, so the active general settings datastore does not provide a path here to update the legacy enum.
The notification actions screen initializes from `messageActionsOrder` and `messageActionsCutoff`, and persists only those two fields. It does not read or update `notificationQuickDeleteBehaviour`.

The newer notification preference model contains both `messageActionsOrder`/`messageActionsCutoff` and the legacy `notificationQuickDeleteBehaviour`, whose default is `NotificationQuickDelete.ALWAYS`.
Confirmed storage keys: `messageActionsOrder`, `messageActionsCutoff`, `isSummaryDeleteActionEnabled`, and legacy `notificationQuickDelete` are separate preferences.

The manager loads `notificationQuickDelete` from storage and saves it back. It derives summary delete from the legacy enum only when the newer summary key is absent, but does not migrate or clear `notificationQuickDelete` for single-message actions.
Confirmed `NotificationQuickDelete` enum values are `ALWAYS`, `FOR_SINGLE_MSG`, and `NEVER`; `NEVER` is a stored state capable of disabling delete.
The settings UI includes `MessageNotificationAction.Delete` mapped to token `NotificationActionTokens.DELETE`, so the user can place Delete in the visible action order.
The screen lets the user reorder actions and move the cutoff; it represents hidden/shown state based on cutoff, not on `notificationQuickDeleteBehaviour`.
This activity simply hosts `NotificationActionsSettingsFragment`; it has no logic to change quick-delete behavior.

The notification settings section exposes `notification_actions_settings` and `notification_summary_delete`, but no active `notification_quick_delete` ListPreference. This supports the report that K-9 settings cannot change the inherited legacy enum.
Found `GeneralSettingsFragment` registers a click listener for `notification_actions_settings` and launches `NotificationActionsSettingsActivity`.

Traced notification data flow: `NewMailNotificationManager` creates `SingleNotificationData`, `SingleMessageNotificationCreator` adds actions from that list, and delete is only absent if filtered out before creation.
Confirmed the new settings screen and notification data creator share the same persisted action-order tokens, including the delete token.

`onCreatePreferencesFix()` sets the preference data store, loads `R.xml.general_settings`, and starts `NotificationActionsSettingsActivity` for `notification_actions_settings`. No quick-delete update occurs in this entry-point path.
`setDeviceActions()` iterates over `notificationData.actions`; if `NotificationAction.Delete` is present it calls `addDeleteAction()`. Therefore the creator is not the suppressing component.
New mail notification creation delegates action selection to `SingleMessageNotificationDataCreator.createSingleNotificationData()`, confirming the faulty decision occurs before builder creation.
Tests verify Delete appears when the action cutoff includes it, but no test sets `NotificationQuickDelete.NEVER`; the fake manager has an unused helper to set that enum.
Found local references to legacy quick-delete storage, values arrays, strings, and tests, but no active `general_settings.xml` preference entry for `notification_quick_delete`.
Confirmed token `delete` is part of the default order and normalization appends supported actions, so the new order mechanism can represent Delete correctly.
Confirmed `SingleNotificationData.actions` is the source list consumed by the notification builder and contains enum value `NotificationAction.Delete` when not filtered out.

The configured Delete action flows correctly until `resolveActions()` receives `isDeleteEnabled = false` from the legacy quick-delete guard; then `NotificationAction.Delete -> isDeleteEnabled` removes Delete from the final action list.

Not the primary fault statement: it correctly persists the new action order and cutoff. Its failure to update the legacy enum matters only because notification generation still incorrectly consults that stale enum.
Not the primary fault statement: when Delete reaches the builder, it creates a delete pending intent and adds the action.

Most likely fault: the statement `return notificationSettings.notificationQuickDeleteBehaviour != NotificationQuickDelete.NEVER` preserves an obsolete disabling condition after Delete became user-configurable via notification action order.

fault_candidates.reasoning: This statement makes the generated single-message notification action list depend on the legacy `notificationQuickDelete` enum. If an upgraded K-9 profile has `notificationQuickDelete = NEVER`, `isDeleteActionEnabled()` returns false; `createSingleNotificationActions()` passes that false to `resolveActions()`; and the `NotificationAction.Delete -> isDeleteEnabled` branch filters Delete out even when the user has placed Delete above the cutoff in the new Notification actions settings screen. The K-9 settings path persists only `messageActionsOrder` and `messageActionsCutoff`, so the user cannot clear this stale disabling condition from the exposed UI.

final_answer.causal_chain: K-9 settings opens `notification_actions_settings`, whose fragment persists only `messageActionsOrder` and `messageActionsCutoff`. When a new mail notification is generated, `NewMailNotificationManager` delegates to `SingleMessageNotificationDataCreator.createSingleNotificationData()`. That calls `createSingleNotificationActions()`, which parses the configured action order and cutoff but also passes `isDeleteEnabled = isDeleteActionEnabled()`. With inherited `notificationQuickDelete = NEVER`, the statement at line 123 returns false. `resolveActions()` then evaluates `NotificationAction.Delete -> isDeleteEnabled` and removes Delete from the action list. `SingleMessageNotificationCreator.setDeviceActions()` therefore never receives `NotificationAction.Delete`, so no delete button appears despite the user selecting it in Notification actions settings.
final_answer.fix_hint: Remove the legacy `notificationQuickDeleteBehaviour != NEVER` gate from single-message notification action resolution, or migrate/update that legacy preference when the new Notification actions settings include Delete.
